---
title: "4.13 Implementing 2D Array Algorithms"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Implementing 2D Array Algorithms

### Reuse 1D patterns inside nested traversal

This topic turns familiar array algorithms into <span class="term">2D array</span> algorithms.

::: {.tight-list}
- the goal is still to search, count, total, compare, or rearrange values
- the main change is that traversal now needs <span class="term">nested loops</span>
- row and column boundaries decide the part of the structure we are allowed to process
:::

## Pattern Shift

### The algorithm idea stays the same, but the traversal shape changes

```java
for (int row = 0; row < array.length; row++)
{
    for (int col = 0; col < array[0].length; col++)
    {
        // use array[row][col]
    }
}
```

Retained teaching point:

::: {.tight-list}
- 1D logic becomes 2D by wrapping it inside an outer row loop
- indexed `for` loops are usually preferred because they expose `row` and `col`
- enhanced `for` can work when exact positions are not needed
:::

## Sum, Average, Min, Max

### Accumulator and comparison patterns work across the whole grid

Core patterns retained from the textbook:

::: {.tight-list}
- keep a running `total` for sums and averages
- initialize `min` or `max` from a real element such as `arr[0][0]`
- update the tracked value when a better candidate appears
- the same logic can target the whole array or one row or one column
:::

## Working with a Subsection

### Row and column ranges let us process only part of the 2D array

```java
for (int row = rowStart; row <= rowEnd; row++)
{
    for (int col = colStart; col <= colEnd; col++)
    {
        if (a[row][col] == value) { count++; }
    }
}
```

This is the retained pattern for questions about a <span class="term">designated row, column, or subsection</span>.

## Property and Duplicate Checks

### AP questions often ask whether values satisfy a condition

Retained uses:

::: {.tight-list}
- determine whether <span class="mark">at least one</span> element has a property
- determine whether <span class="mark">all</span> elements have a property
- count how many elements satisfy a property
- detect duplicate values by comparing pairs reached through systematic traversal
:::

## Rotate and Reverse

### Rearrangement algorithms usually stay local to a row or a column

The textbook keeps these transformations:

::: {.tight-list}
- shift or rotate elements <span class="term">left/right</span> within a row
- shift or rotate elements <span class="term">up/down</span> within a column
- reverse the order of elements in one row or one column
- these are still ordered-movement problems, so index control matters
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- convert a 1D algorithm into a nested-loop 2D algorithm
- total a row, total a column, and compare cells to a tracked min or max
- count values inside a defined subsection
- test whether some or all cells satisfy a property
- explain how duplicate checks and rotation patterns depend on index control
:::

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain that 2D algorithms reuse 1D patterns inside nested loops
- distinguish whole-array traversal from subsection traversal
- describe how to track sum, average, min, or max in a 2D array
- explain the difference between checking any element and checking all elements
- identify why row and column indices matter for rotate and reverse algorithms
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
